title: mathmodels - A Powerful Toolkit for Mathematical Modeling
text: Welcome mathmodels package! comprehensive user-friendly R toolkit designed practitioners researchers field mathematical modeling. core, mathmodels embodies philosophy “simplifying complex,” encapsulating sophisticated mathematical algorithms intuitive functions. allows focus model construction interpretation, rather getting bogged implementation details. mathmodels package developed companion book “Mathematical Modeling: Algorithms Programming Implementation” (China Machine Press). current version (0.0.6) focuses evaluation algorithms, offering complete suite solutions data preprocessing comprehensive evaluation.

title: mathmodels - A Powerful Toolkit for Mathematical Modeling
text: Comprehensive Rigorous Algorithms: package covers wide range mainstream evaluation analysis algorithms, including Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), Entropy Weighting, CRITIC, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) weighting, Weight Combination methods, Technique Order Preference Similarity Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), Grey Relational Analysis, Rank Sum Ratio (RSR), Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation, Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA), Inequality Measures (Gini coefficient, Theil index), System Evaluation (Coupling Coordination Degree, Obstacle Degree), Grey Prediction Models. Elegant Interface, Extremely User-Friendly: Functions designed consistency, featuring standardized parameter names. Many data preprocessing steps, normalization, scaling, indicator direction adjustment, intelligently handled internally, significantly streamlining workflow. Seamless Integration Tidyverse: integrates perfectly |> pipe operations tidyverse tools like dplyr tidyr, making data manipulation batch analysis smooth natural.

title: mathmodels - A Powerful Toolkit for Mathematical Modeling
text: start using mathmodels, first ensure ’s installed loaded: loaded, can immediately use functions. example, perform quick entropy weighting TOPSIS analysis using built-water_quality dataset: Calculate weights using Entropy Method (normalization handled internally). code: AHP(A)

text: numeric matrix, .e. pairwise comparison matrix

title: AHP: Analytic Hierarchy Process — AHP
text: list object contains: w (Weight vector), CR (Consistency ratio), Lmax (Maximum eigenvalue), CI (Consistency index)

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: Combines multiple prediction results (e.g., grey prediction, time series, machine learning models) single prediction using similarity-based weighting approach, improving prediction accuracy.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
code: combine_preds(x)

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: x Numeric vector, prediction results combined (length >= 2).

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: list two elements: : Numeric, combined prediction value. w: Numeric vector, weights prediction x, summing 1.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: function combines prediction results constructing similarity matrix based cosine transformation pairwise differences. Weights derived principal eigenvector similarity matrix, ensuring predictions closer higher influence. two predictions, equal weights (0.5, 0.5) used. predictions identical, equal weights assigned. Compatible mathmodels package enhancing prediction models, including grey prediction, time series, ensemble machine learning.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
code: # Example: Combine three prediction results preds = c(100, 102, 98)  # E.g., from grey prediction, ARIMA, or ML models combine_preds(preds) #> $a #> [1] 100 #>  #> $w #> [1] 0.4142136 0.2928932 0.2928932 #>

what: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: Combines subjective objective weights using linear, multiplicative, game theory-based methods (geometric mean linear system).

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
code: combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "linear", alpha = 0.5)

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: w_subj Numeric vector subjective weights. w_obj Numeric vector objective weights. type Character string specifying combination method: "linear", "multiplicative", "game", "game_linear". alpha Numeric value 0 1, used linear method weight subjective weights. Defaults 0.5.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: numeric vector combined weights, normalized sum 1.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: function supports four methods: Linear: Combines weights alpha * w_subj + (1 - alpha) * w_obj. Multiplicative: Combines weights w_subj * w_obj, requiring positive weights. Game: Uses geometric mean (sqrt(w_subj * w_obj)) balance weights. Game_linear: Uses game-theoretic approach solving linear system based cross-product weights.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
code: w_subj = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1) w_obj = c(0.25, 0.2, 0.3, 0.25) combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "linear", alpha = 0.6) #> [1] 0.34 0.26 0.24 0.16 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "multiplicative") #> [1] 0.4081633 0.2448980 0.2448980 0.1020408 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "game") #> [1] 0.3279556 0.2540333 0.2540333 0.1639778 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "game_linear") #> [1] 0.3735683 0.2823789 0.2176211 0.1264317

what: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
title: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
text: compute_mf transforms single indicator value fuzzy membership vector, element represents degree membership specific evaluation level. compute_mf_funs returns list membership functions visualization purposes.

title: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
code: compute_mf_funs(thresholds)  compute_mf(x, thresholds)

title: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
text: thresholds numeric vector containing least two threshold values define boundaries evaluation levels. x numeric scalar representing value indicator.

title: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
text: list two elements: mv numeric vector, membership degrees level. mfs list functions, one per level, plotting membership functions.

title: Compute fuzzy membership vector and return corresponding membership functions. — compute_mf
code: # Example: SO2 concentration = 0.07, thresholds = c(0.05, 0.15, 0.25, 0.5) th = c(0.05, 0.15, 0.25, 0.5) compute_mf(0.07, th) #> [1] 0.8 0.2 0.0 0.0  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ mfs = compute_mf_funs(th) plots = lapply(mfs, \(x) plot_mf(x, xlim = c(0, 0.6))) gridExtra::grid.arrange(grobs = plots, nrow = 2) } # }

title: CRITIC Weight Method — critic_weight
text: Computes objective weights indicators scores samples using CRITIC method. method considers contrast intensity (e.g., standard deviation entropy) conflict among indicators (based correlation) determine indicator importance. version supports different methods contrast intensity correlation types.

title: CRITIC Weight Method — critic_weight
code: critic_weight(   X,   index = NULL,   method = "std",   cor_method = "pearson",   epsilon = 0.002 )

title: CRITIC Weight Method — critic_weight
text: X numeric data frame matrix rows represent samples (observations) columns represent indicators (variables). index character vector indicating direction indicator: Use "+" positive indicators (higher better), "-" negative indicators (lower better), NA already normalized indicators (rescaling applied).   method Character scalar; specifies method used compute contrast intensity. Options: "std" (standard deviation, default), "entropy" (based information redundancy). cor_method Character scalar; specifies method computing correlations. Options: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "kendall". epsilon small constant used replace exact 0s 1s data prevent log(0) errors. Default 0.002. used method = "entropy".
code: If `index = NULL` (default), all indicators are treated as `NA`, meaning no normalization is performed.

text: Computes standard super-efficiency Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA) models (CCR, BCC, slacks-based), including efficiency scores, slacks, lambdas, targets, returns scale, references, support undesirable outputs. Additionally, calculates Malmquist productivity index measure productivity changes time, including efficiency change (EC), technical change (TC), decomposition components.

title: Data Envelopment Analysis and Malmquist Productivity Index — DEA
text: data DEA functions (basic_DEA, super_DEA, basic_SBM, super_SBM): data frame first column contains DMU (Decision Making Unit) names/identifiers, subsequent columns input/output variables. malmquist: long-format data frame period column, DMU identifiers, input/output variables. inputs numeric vector column indices character vector column names indicating input variables. outputs numeric vector column indices character vector column names indicating (desirable) output variables. ud_outputs Optional. numeric vector indicating position undesirable outputs within outputs parameter. Defaults NULL. applicable super_SBM malmquist. orientation Character string. Model orientation: "io" (input-oriented, default DEA functions) "oo" (output-oriented, default malmquist). rts Character string. Returns scale assumption: "vrs" (variable returns scale, default) "crs" (constant returns scale). period malmquist : numeric character index/name indicating column data containing time periods. type1 malmquist : Reference technology Malmquist index: "cont" (contemporary), "seq" (sequential), "glob" (global, default). type2 malmquist : Decomposition method Malmquist index: "fgnz" (Färe et al., 1994), "rd" (Ray Desli, 1997, default), "gl" (generalized), "bias" (biased).

title: Data Envelopment Analysis and Malmquist Productivity Index — DEA
text: DEA functions (basic_DEA, super_DEA, basic_SBM, super_SBM): list containing six elements: efficiencies: named numeric vector efficiency scores DMU, standardized (0, 1] input- output-oriented models. slacks: data frame matrix containing slack values inputs outputs (including undesirable outputs, specified). lambdas: matrix data frame intensity variables (\(\lambda\)), representing contribution reference DMUs efficiency frontier (self-excluded super-efficiency models). targets: data frame matrix efficient target values inputs outputs, adjusted undesirable outputs DDF models. returns: character vector indicating returns--scale status DMU: "crs" (constant), "irs" (increasing), "drs" (decreasing). references: matrix data frame listing reference DMUs (peers) contributing efficiency frontier (\(\lambda > 0\)). malmquist: data frame containing: Period: Time period transitions (e.g., "t~t+1"). DMU: Decision Making Unit identifiers. mi: Malmquist productivity index, measuring total productivity change. ec: Efficiency change (EC), computed directly mi / tc available model. tc: Technical change (TC), measuring frontier shift. pech: Pure efficiency change (applicable, based decomposition method). sech: Scale efficiency change (applicable, based decomposition method).

title: Data Envelopment Analysis and Malmquist Productivity Index — DEA
text: package provides unified interface computing efficiency scores productivity changes using deaR package. includes five functions: basic_DEA, super_DEA, basic_SBM, super_SBM, malmquist, tailored specific DEA productivity analysis models. DEA Models: basic_DEA: Implements standard radial DEA models (CCR CRS, BCC VRS) described Charnes et al. (1978) Banker et al. (1984), optimizing radial efficiency (input contraction output expansion). super_DEA: Computes super-efficiency radial DEA, excluding evaluated DMU reference set allow efficiency scores beyond 1 (output-oriented) 1 (input-oriented) efficient DMUs (Andersen & Petersen, 1993). basic_SBM: Implements standard Slacks-Based Measure (SBM) models (Tone, 2001), optimizing input output slacks non-radial efficiency measure. super_SBM: Combines SBM super-efficiency properties, excluding evaluated DMU reference set. Note: super_SBM support undesirable outputs. Malmquist Productivity Index: malmquist: Calculates Malmquist productivity index measure productivity changes time, decomposing efficiency change (EC) technical change (TC), optional decomposition pure efficiency change (PECH) scale efficiency change (SECH) based type2 (Färe et al., 1994; Ray & Desli, 1997). EC unavailable (e.g., rts = "vrs" type1 = "glob"), computed ec = mi / tc ensure consistent output. Orientation: Input-oriented ("io"): Minimizes inputs maintaining outputs. Efficiency scores \((0, 1]\) (\(\theta \leq 1\) radial models, \(\rho\) \(\delta \leq 1\) SBM models). Output-oriented ("oo"): Maximizes outputs given inputs. Efficiency scores \((0, 1]\). Radial models output \(\eta \geq 1\), converted \(1/\eta\); SBM models output \(1/\rho^*\) \(1/\delta\). Returns Scale (RTS): CRS ("crs"): Assumes constant returns scale, suitable long-run analysis. VRS ("vrs"): Allows variable returns scale, increasing ("irs") decreasing ("drs") returns determined sum intensity variables (\(\lambda\)). Undesirable Outputs: Supported basic_DEA, super_DEA, basic_SBM using directional distance functions (DDF) direction vector \((g_y, -g_b)\) increase desirable outputs decrease undesirable outputs (Färe & Grosskopf, 2004). supported super_SBM malmquist. Malmquist-Specific Parameters: type1: Defines reference technology Malmquist index: "cont": Contemporary technology, using period's frontier. "seq": Sequential technology, incorporating prior periods. "glob": Global technology, using single frontier across periods. type2: Specifies decomposition method: "fgnz": Färe et al. (1994) decomposition. "rd": Ray Desli (1997) decomposition. "gl": Generalized decomposition. "bias": Bias-corrected decomposition. Handling NA Values: Super-efficiency models (super_DEA, super_SBM) may return NA efficient DMUs due infeasible linear programming solutions, especially VRS (Andersen & Petersen, 1993). Users can replace NA standard efficiency scores exclude affected DMUs. malmquist, ec NULL (e.g., rts = "vrs" type1 = "glob"), computed mi / tc ensure complete result. package leverages deaR robust computation, handling zero values internally ensuring compatibility input/output specifications. Efficiency scores standardized \((0, 1]\) DEA models, Malmquist results formatted data frame easy analysis.

title: Data Envelopment Analysis and Malmquist Productivity Index — DEA

Proceed with caution, some DMUs results may be missing! #> Warning: We note that we call 'targets' to the 'efficient projections' in the #>           strongly efficient frontier. result$efficiencies #>     DMU1     DMU2     DMU3     DMU4     DMU5  #> 1.450000 1.007812 1.000000 1.040000       NA   # Sample Malmquist data (long format) data_malm = data.frame(   DMU = rep(paste0("DMU", 1:5), 3),   Period = rep(1:3, each = 5),   input1 = c(10, 20, 15, 25, 30, 12, 22, 17, 27, 32, 14, 24, 19, 29, 34),   input2 = c(5, 8, 7, 10, 12, 6, 9, 8, 11, 13, 7, 10, 9, 12, 14),   output = c(100, 150, 120, 180, 200, 110, 160, 130, 190, 210, 120, 170, 140, 200, 220) ) malmquist(data_malm, period = 2, inputs = 3:4, outputs = 5) #> # A tibble: 10 × 7 #>    Period DMU      mi    ec    tc  pech  sech #>    <chr>  <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #>  1 1~2    DMU1  0.917 0.922 0.994  1    0.922 #>  2 1~2    DMU2  0.948 0.972 0.976  1    0.972 #>  3 1~2    DMU3  0.948 0.953 0.995  1.00 0.949 #>  4 1~2    DMU4  0.949 0.949 1      1    0.949 #>  5 1~2    DMU5  0.923 0.923 1      1    0.923 #>  6 2~3    DMU1  0.936 0.940 0.995  1    0.940 #>  7 2~3    DMU2  0.976 0.979 0.998  1    0.979 #>  8 2~3    DMU3  0.958 0.962 0.996  1.00 0.958 #>  9 2~3    DMU4  0.955 0.955 1      1    0.955 #> 10 2~3    DMU5  0.929 0.929 1      1    0.929

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: w Numeric vector, factor weights (e.g., combine_weights_linear). R Numeric matrix, fuzzy evaluation matrix columns factors rows evaluation grades. Values 0, 1. type Integer character (1-5), specifying fuzzy composition operator: 1: Min-max (main factor decisive). 2: Product-max (main factor prominent). 3: Weighted sum (additive average). 4: Bounded sum mins (min-sum bounded). 5: Normalized min-sum (balanced average).

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: numeric vector normalized comprehensive evaluation results, summing 1.

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: function computes fuzzy comprehensive evaluation vector B based weight vector w fuzzy evaluation matrix R. Five composition operators supported: Type 1 (min-max): max(min(w, R[j,])), emphasizes main factor. Type 2 (product-max): max(w * R[j,]), highlights main factor. Type 3 (weighted sum): sum(w * R[j,]), additive average. Type 4 (bounded sum): min(1, sum(min(w, R[j,]))), bounds sum mins. Type 5 (normalized min-sum): sum(min(w, R[j,]/sum(R[j,]))), balanced average. output B normalized sum 1. sum zero, error thrown. Uses base R lightweight implementation.

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: collection functions preprocess numeric data, including standardization, L2 norm normalization, Min-Max scaling, centered-type normalization, interval-type normalization, extreme-value-based normalization, initial-value-based normalization, mean-based normalization, negative--positive transformation. functions transform numeric vector standardized normalized scale, suitable various indicator types (positive, negative, centered, interval-based, extreme-based).

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
code: standardize(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)  normalize(x)  rescale(x, type = "+", a = 0, b = 1)  rescale_middle(x, m)  rescale_interval(x, a, b)  rescale_extreme(x, type = "+")  rescale_initial(x, type = "+")  rescale_mean(x)  to_positive(x, type = "minmax")

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: x Numeric vector preprocessed. center Logical numeric scalar, passed base::scale centering (standardize). Default TRUE. scale Logical numeric scalar, passed base::scale scaling (standardize). Default TRUE. type Character scalar specifying transformation direction method: "+" Positive direction (larger values better, rescale, rescale_extreme rescale_initial). "-" Negative direction (smaller values better, rescale rescale_extreme rescale_initial). "minmax" Min-max transformation (to_positive). "reciprocal" Reciprocal transformation (to_positive). Numeric scalar, lower bound output range optimal interval (rescale rescale_interval). b Numeric scalar, upper bound output range optimal interval (rescale rescale_interval). m Numeric scalar, optimal value centered-type normalization (rescale_middle).

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: numeric vector length x, transformed follows: standardize: Standardized values (mean = 0, sd = 1). normalize: L2 norm normalized values (Euclidean norm, unit length). rescale: Min-Max scaled values [, b] (default 0, 1). rescale_middle: Centered-type normalized values 0, 1, 1 indicates x = m. rescale_interval: Interval-type normalized values 0, 1, 1 indicates x [, b]. rescale_extreme: Extreme-based normalized values using min(x)/x (positive) x/max(x) (negative). rescale_initial: Initial-based normalized values using x/x[1] x[1]/x. rescale_mean: Mean-based normalized values using x/mean(x). to_positive: Transformed values converting negative indicators positive using min-max reciprocal transformation.

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: functions support various preprocessing needs data analysis: standardize: Applies Z-score standardization (mean = 0, sd = 1), ideal equalizing variances normally distributed data. normalize: Scales vector unit length dividing L2 (Euclidean) norm, useful machine learning similarity calculations. rescale: Performs Min-Max scaling specified range (default 0, 1), supporting positive negative indicators. rescale_middle: Normalizes centered-type indicators, values closer optimal value m better, mapping 0, 1. rescale_interval: Normalizes interval-type indicators, values within [, b] optimal, mapping 0, 1. rescale_extreme: Normalizes using extreme values: min(x)/x positive indicators x/max(x) negative indicators, often used grey relational analysis. rescale_initial: Normalizes dividing first value (x/x[1] x[1]/x), commonly used grey relational analysis. rescale_mean: Normalizes dividing mean (x/mean(x)), commonly used grey relational analysis. to_positive: Converts negative indicators positive using either min-max (max(x) - x) reciprocal (1/x) transformation. Missing values (NA) preserved output. rescale_initial rescale_mean, initial value mean must non-zero, respectively.

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
code: # Standardization x = c(4, 1, NA, 5, 8) standardize(x) #> [1] -0.1732051 -1.2124356         NA  0.1732051  1.2124356  # L2 norm normalization normalize(x) #> [1] 0.38851434 0.09712859         NA 0.48564293 0.77702869  # Min-Max normalization (positive direction) rescale(x)                # Scale to [0, 1] #> [1] 0.4285714 0.0000000        NA 0.5714286 1.0000000 rescale(x, type = "-", a = 0.002, b = 0.996)  # Reverse scaling #> [1] 0.570 0.996    NA 0.428 0.002  # Negative-to-positive transformation to_positive(x)                       # Min-max transformation #> [1]  4  7 NA  3  0 to_positive(x, type = "reciprocal")  # Reciprocal transformation #> [1] 0.250 1.000    NA 0.200 0.125  # Centered-type normalization PH = 6:9 rescale_middle(PH, 7) #> [1] 0.5 1.0 0.5 0.0  # Interval-type normalization Temp = c(35.2, 35.8, 36.6, 37.1, 37.8, 38.4) rescale_interval(Temp, 36, 37) #> [1] 0.4285714 0.8571429 1.0000000 0.9285714 0.4285714 0.0000000  # Extreme-based normalization rescale_extreme(x)         # min(x)/x #> [1] 0.500 0.125    NA 0.625 1.000 rescale_extreme(x, "-")    # x/max(x) #> [1] 0.250 1.000    NA 0.200 0.125  # Initial-based normalization rescale_initial(x) #> [1] 1.00 0.25   NA 1.25 2.00  # Mean-based normalization rescale_mean(x) #> [1] 0.8888889 0.2222222        NA 1.1111111 1.7777778

title: mathmodels 0.0.6
text: AHP(): Extended Random Index (RI) table n = 11 n = 15, added validation n > 15. Previously, AHP() silently return NA consistency ratios 11 criteria. grey_corr(): Fixed incorrect default weight direction internal validation; weights now correctly validated nrow(cmp) (number evaluation objects) rather ncol(cmp) (number indicators). DGM21(): Fixed list(pred = fitted, ...) return value fitted undefined; corrected list(fitted = pred, ...). Also fixed initialization value x0 hard-coded constant first element input series X[1]. grey_corr_topsis(): Fixed internal transpose error call grey_corr() caused dimension mismatch non-square matrices. z_mf(), pi_mf(), s_mf(): Rewrote two-stage spline algorithm three membership functions. original implementation used single-stage approximation logically unreachable second-phase conditions, causing values fall outside valid [0, 1] range certain parameter configurations. fix ensures proper two-stage spline behavior exact midpoint values 0.5.
